summary:
The Saka Kingdoms (c. 400–200 BC) were eastern Iranian nomadic peoples closely related to the Scythians, who roamed Central Asia from the Aral Sea to the Tarim Basin, interacting with the Persian, Hellenistic, and Indian worlds and eventually establishing kingdoms in the northwestern Indian subcontinent as the [[event:1215|Indo-Scythian Kingdom]].

The Saka (known as Sacae to Greeks and Romans) are mentioned in Persian royal inscriptions as subjects of the Achaemenid Empire. Their territory straddled the crucial routes connecting Persia with Central Asia and the [[event:1129|Silk Road]] corridors. After the [[event:1173|Greco-Bactrian Kingdom]] was weakened by the Yuezhi migration in the 2nd century BC, the Saka moved into Bactria and later into Arachosia and northwestern India. The Saka/Scythian presence in India, the [[event:1215|Indo-Scythian Kingdom]], lasted from around 200 BC to 400 AD and left significant cultural traces — including a Saka calendar still used in India (the national calendar of independent India is based on a reformed Saka era). Saka burial sites across Central Asia have yielded rich material culture comparable to the related Scythian tradition.
